_note: SAMPLE free-response questions with correct physics. difficulty is 1 (easy) – 3 (hard); points are per part. courseId must match a course id in registry.json (badge color comes from the registry). Replace/expand freely.

questions.title: Projectile launched from a cliff
questions.scenario: A ball is launched horizontally at 15 m/s from the top of a 20 m cliff. Ignore air resistance and take g = 9.8 m/s².

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: How long is the ball in the air before it lands?

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: How far from the base of the cliff does it land?

parts.label: Part (c)
parts.text: What is the ball's speed just before impact?

questions.title: Block on an inclined plane
questions.scenario: A 4.0 kg block is released from rest on a frictionless incline angled at 30° above the horizontal.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Draw a free-body diagram and find the acceleration down the incline.
parts.modelAnswer: Forces: weight mg down, normal force N perpendicular. a = g·sin30° = 9.8 × 0.5 = 4.9 m/s².

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: If the incline is 2.0 m long, how fast is the block moving at the bottom?

questions.title: Spring launches a cart
questions.scenario: A spring (k = 800 N/m) is compressed 0.10 m and used to launch a 0.50 kg cart along a frictionless track.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: How much elastic potential energy is stored in the compressed spring?

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: What is the cart's launch speed?

questions.title: Perfectly inelastic collision
questions.scenario: A 1200 kg car moving east at 20 m/s collides with a stationary 800 kg car and they lock together.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the velocity of the combined wreck immediately after the collision.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: How much kinetic energy is lost in the collision?

questions.title: Car on a flat curve
questions.scenario: A car rounds a flat (unbanked) curve of radius 50 m. The coefficient of static friction between the tires and road is μₛ = 0.60. Take g = 9.8 m/s².

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Which force provides the centripetal force, and what is the condition for the maximum speed?
parts.modelAnswer: Static friction provides the centripetal force. At the maximum speed friction is at its max: μₛ·m·g = m·v²/r.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Calculate the maximum speed for which the car does not skid.

questions.title: Mass on a spring
questions.scenario: A 0.50 kg mass oscillates on a spring with k = 200 N/m and amplitude 0.10 m on a frictionless surface.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the period of oscillation.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Find the maximum speed of the mass.

questions.title: Pressure at depth in a tank
questions.scenario: A water tank is open to the atmosphere. Water density ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and P₀ = 1.0×10⁵ Pa.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the absolute pressure 3.0 m below the surface.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: A 0.020 m² hatch sits at that depth. What net force must its hinges withstand from the water gauge pressure?
parts.modelAnswer: Gauge pressure = ρ·g·h = 29400 Pa. F = P·A = 29400 × 0.020 ≈ 588 N.

questions.title: Heating a gas at constant volume
questions.scenario: 2.0 mol of an ideal gas is held in a rigid container and 500 J of heat is added.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: How much work does the gas do on its surroundings?
parts.modelAnswer: Constant volume → ΔV = 0, so W = 0. The gas does no work.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Using the first law, find the change in the gas's internal energy.
parts.modelAnswer: ΔU = Q + W_on = 500 + 0 = 500 J. Internal energy increases by 500 J.

questions.title: Force between two point charges
questions.scenario: Two point charges, q₁ = +3.0 µC and q₂ = −5.0 µC, are 0.20 m apart. k = 8.99×10⁹ N·m²/C².

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the magnitude of the electric force between them.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: State whether the force is attractive or repulsive and why.
parts.modelAnswer: Attractive — the charges have opposite signs, so they pull toward each other.

questions.title: Series resistor circuit
questions.scenario: A 12 V battery is connected to a 4.0 Ω resistor and an 8.0 Ω resistor in series.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the current through the circuit.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Find the power dissipated in the 8.0 Ω resistor.

questions.title: Charged particle in a magnetic field
questions.scenario: A proton (q = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C, m = 1.67×10⁻²⁷ kg) moves at 2.0×10⁶ m/s perpendicular to a 0.50 T magnetic field.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic force on the proton.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Calculate the radius of its circular path.

questions.title: Work done by a variable force
questions.scenario: A force F(x) = 6x (in newtons, x in meters) acts on a 2.0 kg object moving along the x-axis from x = 0 to x = 3.0 m, starting from rest.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the work done by the force over this interval.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Use the work–energy theorem to find the object's speed at x = 3.0 m.

questions.title: Torque and angular acceleration of a disk
questions.scenario: A uniform solid disk (I = ½·M·R²) has M = 3.0 kg and R = 0.40 m. A constant tangential force of 12 N is applied at its rim.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the moment of inertia and the net torque about the center.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Determine the angular acceleration of the disk.

questions.title: Solid sphere rolling down an incline
questions.scenario: A uniform solid sphere (I = ⅖·M·R²) rolls without slipping from rest down an incline, descending a vertical height h = 2.0 m. Take g = 9.8 m/s².

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Using energy conservation with the rolling constraint, derive the speed at the bottom.
parts.modelAnswer: M·g·h = ½·M·v² + ½·I·ω², with ω = v/R and I = ⅖MR². This gives M·g·h = ½Mv² + ⅕Mv² = 7/10·M·v² → v = √(10·g·h/7).

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Evaluate the speed for h = 2.0 m.

questions.title: Disk dropped onto a spinning disk
questions.scenario: A disk with I₁ = 4.0 kg·m² spins freely at ω₁ = 3.0 rad/s. A second, non-rotating disk with I₂ = 2.0 kg·m² is dropped coaxially onto it and they rotate together.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the final common angular velocity.
parts.modelAnswer: Conserve angular momentum: I₁ω₁ = (I₁+I₂)ω → ω = (4.0×3.0)/(6.0) = 2.0 rad/s.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: How much rotational kinetic energy is lost?
parts.modelAnswer: K_i = ½·4.0·3.0² = 18 J; K_f = ½·6.0·2.0² = 12 J. Lost = 6.0 J (to friction between disks).

questions.title: SHM from a position function
questions.scenario: A particle undergoes simple harmonic motion described by x(t) = 0.05·cos(10t), with x in meters and t in seconds.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find v(t) and a(t) by differentiation.
parts.modelAnswer: v = dx/dt = −0.50·sin(10t) m/s; a = dv/dt = −5.0·cos(10t) m/s².

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: State the maximum speed and maximum acceleration.

questions.title: Field of a charged sphere
questions.scenario: A solid insulating sphere of radius R carries total charge Q uniformly distributed. Use Gauss's law.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Derive the electric field outside the sphere (r > R).
parts.modelAnswer: By symmetry E·4πr² = Q/ε₀ → E = Q / (4π·ε₀·r²) = k·Q/r², directed radially.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Derive the electric field inside the sphere (r < R).
parts.modelAnswer: Enclosed charge q = Q·(r³/R³). E·4πr² = q/ε₀ → E = k·Q·r / R³, radially outward.

questions.title: Charging an RC circuit
questions.scenario: A 12 V battery charges a capacitor C = 100 µF through a resistor R = 1000 Ω, starting from an uncharged capacitor.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Find the time constant and the initial current.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Find the charge on the capacitor after one time constant.

questions.title: Parallel-plate capacitor energy
questions.scenario: A parallel-plate capacitor has plate area A = 0.010 m² and separation d = 1.0 mm with vacuum between the plates. Use ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² F/m.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the capacitance.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Find the energy stored when charged to 100 V.

questions.title: Magnetic field inside a solenoid
questions.scenario: A long solenoid has n = 1000 turns per meter and carries current I = 2.0 A. Use μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ T·m/A.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Use Ampère's law to derive the field inside a long solenoid.
parts.modelAnswer: Apply ∮B·dl = μ₀·I_enc to a rectangular loop of length L partly inside: B·L = μ₀·(n·L)·I → B = μ₀·n·I (uniform, along the axis; field outside ≈ 0).

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Evaluate the field magnitude.

questions.title: Induced EMF in a shrinking field
questions.scenario: A coil of N = 50 turns encloses an area A = 0.020 m². A uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the coil decreases steadily from 0.50 T to 0 in 0.10 s.

parts.label: Part (a)
parts.text: Calculate the magnitude of the induced EMF.

parts.label: Part (b)
parts.text: Use Lenz's law to state the direction of the induced current.
parts.modelAnswer: The flux is decreasing, so the induced current flows to oppose the decrease — it circulates to maintain the original field direction (i.e., in the sense that reinforces B through the coil).
